content.text: The Enterprise-Scale Landing Zone (ESLZ) reference architecture is Microsoft's opinionated, production-ready implementation of the CAF landing zone design areas. It defines a precise management group hierarchy, a set of 500+ custom and built-in Azure Policy definitions, a hub-spoke network topology with Azure Firewall, and a management baseline with centralized Log Analytics. The architecture is available as open-source Bicep (ALZ-Bicep) and Terraform (ALZ-Terraform) accelerators on GitHub.

attrs.alt: Enterprise-Scale Landing Zone reference architecture — management groups, subscriptions, hub-spoke VNets, policy layer, and management baseline

content.text: The ESLZ Bicep accelerator is not a tutorial — it is production IaC. Fork it, configure the parameters files for your tenant, and deploy it in order: management groups first, then policy definitions, then policy assignments, then subscription placement. Do not hand-edit the generated modules.

The canonical ESLZ management group hierarchy has one top-level MG (alz) below the tenant root, then four children: platform (connectivity, identity, management platform subs), landing-zones (corp for private workloads, online for internet-facing), sandbox (developer experimentation), and decommissioned (subscriptions being retired). This structure allows different baseline policies at each tier without per-subscription assignments.

# Enterprise-Scale management group hierarchy — create via CLI
# Mirrors the canonical ALZ MG structure

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

# Top-level MGs under tenant root
az account management-group create --name 'alz'           --display-name 'Azure Landing Zones' --parent-id $TENANT_ID
az account management-group create --name 'alz-platform'  --display-name 'Platform'            --parent 'alz'
az account management-group create --name 'alz-lz'        --display-name 'Landing Zones'       --parent 'alz'
az account management-group create --name 'alz-sandbox'   --display-name 'Sandbox'             --parent 'alz'
az account management-group create --name 'alz-decom'     --display-name 'Decommissioned'      --parent 'alz'

# Platform sub-MGs
az account management-group create --name 'alz-platform-connectivity' --display-name 'Connectivity' --parent 'alz-platform'
az account management-group create --name 'alz-platform-identity'     --display-name 'Identity'     --parent 'alz-platform'
az account management-group create --name 'alz-platform-management'   --display-name 'Management'   --parent 'alz-platform'

# Landing zone sub-MGs (corp = private connectivity; online = internet-facing)
az account management-group create --name 'alz-lz-corp'   --display-name 'Corp'   --parent 'alz-lz'
az account management-group create --name 'alz-lz-online' --display-name 'Online' --parent 'alz-lz'

# Place platform subscriptions
az account management-group subscription add --name 'alz-platform-connectivity' --subscription '<connectivity-sub-id>'
az account management-group subscription add --name 'alz-platform-identity'     --subscription '<identity-sub-id>'
az account management-group subscription add --name 'alz-platform-management'   --subscription '<management-sub-id>'

# Verify hierarchy
az account management-group show --name 'alz' --expand --recurse \
  --query '{Name:name, Children:children[].{Name:name,Type:type}}' -o json
Parent for all ESLZ MGs; top-level guardrails apply here
Allowed locations, deny public IP (if required), require tags
Deny workload resource types; restrict to platform resource types
Defender for Cloud enabled, diagnostic routing, update management
Private workloads using hub VNet / ExpressRoute / VPN
Require private endpoints, deny internet egress without Firewall, NSG required
Internet-facing workloads (App Gateway, CDN, public-facing apps)
Less restrictive egress; Application Gateway WAF required
Auto-shutdown policies; spending limits; permissive networking

The ALZ-Bicep repository provides a reference implementation in four sequential deployment modules. Clone it, populate the parameter JSON files with your tenant IDs and subscription IDs, then deploy each module in order. The accelerator creates 500+ policy definitions, assigns them at the appropriate management group scopes, and places your platform subscriptions. Do not customize the module code itself — override behaviour via parameter files.

# Clone and configure the ALZ Bicep accelerator
# This is the Microsoft-maintained reference implementation for Enterprise-Scale

git clone https://github.com/Azure/ALZ-Bicep.git
cd ALZ-Bicep

# The accelerator deploys in four ordered modules:
# 1. Management Groups
# 2. Custom Policy Definitions (500+ built-in ALZ policies)
# 3. Policy Assignments
# 4. Subscription Placement

# Step 1: Deploy management group hierarchy
az deployment tenant create \
  --name 'alz-mg-hierarchy' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroups.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroups.parameters.all.json

# Step 2: Deploy ALZ custom policy definitions at the root MG
az deployment mg create \
  --name 'alz-policy-defs' \
  --management-group-id '<tenant-root-mg-id>' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/customPolicyDefinitions.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/customPolicyDefinitions.parameters.all.json

# Step 3: Assign policies at each MG scope
az deployment mg create \
  --name 'alz-policy-assignments' \
  --management-group-id 'alz' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/assignments/alzDefaults/alzDefaultPolicyAssignments.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/assignments/alzDefaults/alzDefaultPolicyAssignments.parameters.all.json

# Step 4: Place platform subscriptions in the correct management groups
az deployment mg create \
  --name 'alz-subscription-placement' \
  --management-group-id 'alz' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/subscriptionPlacement/subscriptionPlacement.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/subscriptionPlacement/subscriptionPlacement.parameters.all.json
Connectivity Subscription: Hub VNet with Azure Firewall Premium

The connectivity platform subscription holds the hub VNet with Azure Firewall Premium (IDPS + TLS inspection), an ExpressRoute or VPN Gateway, Bastion, and Private DNS zones. All spoke VNets peer to this hub and route egress through the Firewall. Azure Firewall Premium is used over Standard in ESLZ because it supports threat intelligence-based filtering and TLS inspection for encrypted outbound traffic.

# Enterprise-Scale connectivity subscription: hub VNet with Azure Firewall Premium
# This is the production-grade platform connectivity pattern

SUB='<connectivity-sub-id>'
az account set --subscription $SUB

RG='rg-connectivity-prod-eastus'
az group create --name $RG --location eastus

# Create hub VNet with ALZ address ranges
az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --address-prefixes 10.0.0.0/16

# Required subnets (names are fixed by Azure)
SUBNETS=("AzureFirewallSubnet:10.0.0.0/26" "AzureFirewallManagementSubnet:10.0.0.64/26" "GatewaySubnet:10.0.1.0/27" "AzureBastionSubnet:10.0.2.0/27")
for entry in "${SUBNETS[@]}"; do
  NAME=${entry%%:*}
  CIDR=${entry##*:}
  az network vnet subnet create \
    --name $NAME \
    --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
    --resource-group $RG \
    --address-prefix $CIDR
done

# Deploy Azure Firewall Premium (required for IDPS and TLS inspection)
az network public-ip create --name pip-afw-hub-prod-eastus --resource-group $RG --sku Standard --zone 1 2 3

az network firewall create \
  --name afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --location eastus \
  --sku-name AZFW_VNet \
  --sku-tier Premium

FW_CONFIG=$(az network firewall ip-config create \
  --firewall-name afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --name FW-config \
  --public-ip-address pip-afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --query 'privateIpAddress' -o tsv)
echo 'Firewall private IP: '$FW_CONFIG

# Deploy ExpressRoute Gateway (Standard SKU — scale up to UltraPerformance for >10Gbps)
az network public-ip create --name pip-vpng-hub-prod-eastus --resource-group $RG --sku Standard --zone 1 2 3

az network vnet-gateway create \
  --name vpng-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --vnet vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --gateway-type ExpressRoute \
  --sku Standard \
  --public-ip-address pip-vpng-hub-prod-eastus \
  --no-wait
echo 'ExpressRoute gateway deploying (takes ~20 minutes)...'

ESLZ assigns policies in tiers that mirror the management group hierarchy. Each tier adds policies specific to its scope. Understanding this layering is essential for debugging 'disallowed by policy' errors: the error message names the assignment but not the management group scope; use az policy assignment show --id <assignmentId> to find the scope.
Allowed regions, require mandatory tags on resource groups, deny Premium storage in dev
Deny non-platform resource types, require public IP only in connectivity sub
Enable Defender CSPM, route activity logs to central Log Analytics, deny unencrypted storage
Deny public endpoints for PaaS services, require private endpoints for Key Vault/Storage/SQL
Require WAF on Application Gateway, deny HTTP (port 80) without redirect to HTTPS
Workload-specific exceptions; e.g. allow a specific public IP for a legacy integration

The identity subscription sits under alz-platform-identity and hosts the centralized authentication infrastructure. In hybrid environments this is either Entra ID Domain Services (managed AD) or customer-managed domain controllers. The identity VNet is peered directly to the hub with its own address range separate from the hub range, allowing domain-joined workloads in spokes to reach domain controllers without traffic leaving the hub perimeter. The identity subscription does not expose resources publicly and is not accessible from the internet.

# Design Area: Identity subscription setup in ESLZ
# The identity subscription hosts Entra ID Domain Services (AADDS) or
# customer-managed Active Directory Domain Controllers for hybrid workloads.

SUB_IDENTITY='<identity-sub-id>'

# Move to alz-platform management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-platform-identity \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY

# Create resource groups for domain controllers
az group create \
  --name rg-identity-prod-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY

# Deploy VNet for domain controllers
# The identity VNet is peered to the hub but has its OWN subnet range.
az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-identity-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-identity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.1.0.0/24 \
  --subnet-name snet-dc-prod-eastus \
  --subnet-prefix 10.1.0.0/27 \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY

# Peer identity VNet to hub (two-directional peering required)
HUB_VNET_ID=$(az network vnet show \
  --name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --subscription '<connectivity-sub-id>' \
  --query id -o tsv)

IDENTITY_VNET_ID=$(az network vnet show \
  --name vnet-identity-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-identity-prod-eastus \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Peer: identity -> hub
az network vnet peering create \
  --name peer-identity-to-hub \
  --vnet-name vnet-identity-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-identity-prod-eastus \
  --remote-vnet $HUB_VNET_ID \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY

# Peer: hub -> identity (must be created in the connectivity subscription)
az network vnet peering create \
  --name peer-hub-to-identity \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --remote-vnet $IDENTITY_VNET_ID \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic \
  --subscription '<connectivity-sub-id>'

# Deploy Azure AD DS (Entra Domain Services) — provides managed domain controllers
az ds domain-service create \
  --name contoso.corp \
  --resource-group rg-identity-prod-eastus \
  --subnet-id "${IDENTITY_VNET_ID}/subnets/snet-dc-prod-eastus" \
  --sku Standard \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY

# Update DNS servers on spoke VNets to point to the domain controllers
# (AADDS provides two DC IPs; retrieve them after provisioning)
DC_IPS=$(az ds domain-service show \
  --name contoso.corp \
  --resource-group rg-identity-prod-eastus \
  --query 'domainControllerIpAddress' -o tsv \
  --subscription $SUB_IDENTITY)

echo "Domain controller IPs: $DC_IPS"
# Set these as custom DNS servers on each spoke VNet that needs domain join capability

The management subscription provisions the monitoring infrastructure that every other subscription routes data into. The Log Analytics workspace is the single pane of glass — Activity Logs, diagnostic settings, security events, and VM performance metrics all flow here. An Automation Account linked to the workspace enables Update Management (patch compliance), Change Tracking (configuration drift), and custom runbooks. Defender for Cloud is configured to use this workspace as its data destination so security alerts are co-located with operational logs.

# Management subscription: full observability stack
# This subscription hosts Log Analytics, Defender for Cloud workspace,
# Azure Monitor, Automation Account, and Update Management center.

SUB_MGMT='<management-sub-id>'
RG='rg-management-prod-eastus'
LOCATION='eastus'

# 1. Central Log Analytics workspace (retention: 90 days)
az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --workspace-name log-management-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --location $LOCATION \
  --sku PerGB2018 \
  --retention-time 90 \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT

LOG_WS_ID=$(az monitor log-analytics workspace show \
  --workspace-name log-management-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT \
  --query id -o tsv)

# 2. Automation Account for runbooks and Update Management
az automation account create \
  --name auto-management-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --location $LOCATION \
  --sku Basic \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT

# Link Automation Account to Log Analytics workspace
az resource create \
  --resource-type Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/linkedServices \
  --name "log-management-prod-eastus/Automation" \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT \
  --properties "{\"resourceId\": \"$(az automation account show \
    --name auto-management-prod-eastus \
    --resource-group $RG \
    --subscription $SUB_MGMT \
    --query id -o tsv)\"}"

# 3. Enable solutions in the workspace
for SOLUTION in Security Updates ChangeTracking VMInsights; do
  az monitor log-analytics solution create \
    --solution-type $SOLUTION \
    --workspace $LOG_WS_ID \
    --resource-group $RG \
    --subscription $SUB_MGMT
done

# 4. Configure Azure Activity Log routing to the workspace
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name route-activity-to-log \
  --resource "/subscriptions/$SUB_MGMT" \
  --logs '[{"category":"Administrative","enabled":true},{"category":"Security","enabled":true},{"category":"Policy","enabled":true},{"category":"Alert","enabled":true}]' \
  --workspace $LOG_WS_ID

# 5. Defender for Cloud: set workspace for all subscriptions
# Run once per subscription that should send data to the management workspace
az security workspace-setting create \
  --name default \
  --target-workspace $LOG_WS_ID \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT
ESLZ Health Monitoring with Resource Graph and KQL

ESLZ architecture health is monitored through two lenses: Azure Resource Graph for resource-level inventory (policy compliance, scope drift, role assignments) and Log Analytics for runtime data (firewall threat intel hits, VM performance, change events). The queries below form the basis of a weekly ESLZ governance report that the platform team reviews to catch drift before it becomes a security or reliability problem.

// ESLZ health check queries — run from Azure Resource Graph or Log Analytics

// 1. Policy compliance across all management groups
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.policyinsights/policystates'
| where tostring(properties.complianceState) =~ 'NonCompliant'
| extend
    policyName       = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName),
    resourceId       = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    mgPath           = tostring(properties.managementGroupIds)
| summarize NonCompliantCount = count() by policyName, mgPath
| order by NonCompliantCount desc
| take 20

// 2. Subscriptions that have drifted outside the ALZ management group tree
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
| where managementGroupAncestorsChain !has 'alz'
| project name, subscriptionId, managementGroupAncestorsChain

// 3. Resources deployed outside approved regions
Resources
| where location !in ('eastus', 'eastus2', 'westus2')
| summarize Count = count() by location, subscriptionId
| order by Count desc

// 4. Management group role assignments (detect standing privileged access)
AuthorizationResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/roleassignments'
| where tostring(properties.scope) contains 'managementGroups'
| extend
    principalId    = tostring(properties.principalId),
    roleDefId      = tostring(properties.roleDefinitionId),
    scope          = tostring(properties.scope)
| where roleDefId endswith '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635'   // Owner
    or roleDefId endswith 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'   // Contributor
| project principalId, roleDefId, scope
| order by scope asc

// 5. Connectivity subscription: Azure Firewall threat intel hits (last 24h)
// Run in Log Analytics workspace, not Resource Graph
AzureDiagnostics
| where Category == 'AzureFirewallThreatIntelLog'
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| summarize Count = count() by Action_s, TargetUrl_s, Protocol_s
| order by Count desc

Teams with an existing Terraform workflow can use the AzureRM Terraform provider instead of ALZ-Bicep to stand up the management group hierarchy. The approach mirrors the Bicep module structure — one resource per management group, with explicit parent_management_group_id chains. Remote state in Azure Blob Storage ensures the hierarchy is managed as code. ALZ-Bicep and Terraform produce equivalent infrastructure; the choice is driven by the team's existing IaC toolchain.

Over time, manually created subscriptions, forgotten route tables, or misconfigured policy assignments accumulate and erode the ESLZ guarantees. A weekly drift-detection script audits three critical properties: all subscriptions are under the ALZ management group root, all resources carry required tags, and all spoke subnets have a UDR that routes 0.0.0.0/0 to the hub Firewall private IP. Subscriptions or resources that fail these checks are reported to the platform team for remediation.

# Detect ESLZ drift: subscriptions or resources that violate the architecture

# 1. Find subscriptions NOT under the ALZ root management group
echo '=== Subscriptions outside ALZ root ==='
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri 'https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz/descendants?api-version=2020-05-01&$skiptoken=&$top=1000' \
  --query 'value[?type==`/subscriptions`].{Name:properties.displayName,Id:name}' \
  -o table

# Cross-reference against all subscriptions in the tenant
az account list --query '[].{Name:name, Id:id}' -o tsv | \
  while read name id; do
    # check if this id appears in the ALZ tree
    IN_ALZ=$(az rest \
      --method GET \
      --uri "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz/descendants?api-version=2020-05-01" \
      --query "length(value[?name=='${id##*/}'])" -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
    if [[ $IN_ALZ -eq 0 ]]; then
      echo "ORPHANED: $name ($id)"
    fi
  done

# 2. Find resources with no tags (governance gap)
echo '=== Resources with no tags (sample per subscription) ==='
for SUB in $(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv); do
  COUNT=$(az resource list \
    --subscription $SUB \
    --query "length([?tags==null || length(keys(tags))==\`0\`])" \
    -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
  if [[ $COUNT -gt 0 ]]; then
    NAME=$(az account show --subscription $SUB --query name -o tsv)
    echo "$NAME ($SUB): $COUNT untagged resources"
  fi
done

# 3. Firewall: verify all spoke traffic routes through hub Firewall
# UDRs in spoke subnets should point 0.0.0.0/0 to the Firewall private IP
echo '=== Route tables missing Firewall default route ==='
az network route-table list --query \
  '[].{RT:name, RG:resourceGroup, Routes:routes[].{Prefix:addressPrefix,NextHop:nextHopIpAddress}}' \
  -o json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
rts = json.load(sys.stdin)
for rt in rts:
    routes = rt.get('Routes') or []
    has_default = any(r.get('Prefix') == '0.0.0.0/0' for r in routes)
    if not has_default:
        print(f\"Missing default route: {rt['RT']} ({rt['RG']})\")
"
Full ESLZ: MG hierarchy, policies, connectivity, management
Teams new to ALZ, Microsoft-first toolchain, quickest path to ESLZ compliance
Teams already using Terraform; integrates with existing Terraform pipeline and state management
Full ESLZ via Azure Portal wizard
Proof-of-concept or single-team deployments; not recommended for production (no IaC audit trail)
Organizations with existing platform that needs partial alignment (e.g. add only the policy layer on top of existing subscriptions)

The Enterprise-Scale Landing Zone reference architecture provides a prescriptive, battle-tested implementation of all eight CAF design areas. The management group hierarchy (alz > platform, landing-zones, sandbox, decommissioned) with the ALZ policy assignment tiers ensures consistent governance without per-subscription manual work. Use the ALZ-Bicep or ALZ-Terraform accelerator as the starting point rather than building from scratch, then customize parameter files for your organization's specific constraints — the module code itself should not be modified.
